original_abstract: While several Chinese cities, including Xiamen, Dalian, and Ningbo, have successfully hosted peaceful anti-PX (para-xylene) protests in recent years, the 2014 demonstrations in Maoming emerged as a violent and noteworthy exception. Drawing on participant observation and in-depth interviews, this article explores why the Maoming movement took such a different trajectory. We find that despite intensive official propaganda, Maoming residents possessed a sophisticated understanding of environmental risks, rooted in their daily experiences with industrial pollution and their awareness of successful activism elsewhere. Unlike earlier middle-class movements, however, the Maoming protest was largely leaderless and spontaneous, a factor that contributed to its volatile nature and the subsequent state repression. Finally, we demonstrate how the movement managed to achieve large-scale mobilization—even without the backing of the middle class or mainstream media—by leveraging encrypted social media channels and a strategic use of the local urban landscape.
humanized_abstract: Chinese cities such as Xiamen, Dalian and Ningbo have in recent years host so called “peaceful” anti-PX (para-xylene) protests, but in 2014 Maoming witnessed a violent protest not seen before. Through participant observation and through interviews with participants to the protest, this article examines the explanations for this violent outbreak. Our findings suggest that even after heavy police propaganda, citizens in Maoming not only fully understood the risks of environmental pollution, but that also, through their everyday experiences and knowledge that they gathered from national media and from information shared on micro blogs they knew that their concerns would be acted upon as they were aware of the other protests against environment-damaging projects in other Chinese cities. Unlike other protests that had a clear group of leaders as also generally attracted a middle class audience and, as such, media coverage, the Maoming protest broke the patterns as it was largely leaderless, and had a very different composition of citizens, all factors which worked in favour of the police in relation to bringing the unrest to an end. Yet, although vastly differing from other protests, the Maomin protest did manage to attract a large number of citizens; using encrypted micro blogs and also using the spaces of the city in a way that they were turned into new locations for political action.

Unlike other protests that had a clear group of leaders as also generally attracted a middle class audience and, as such, media coverage, the Maoming protest broke the patterns as it was largely leaderless, and had a very different composition of citizens, all factors which worked in favour of the police in relation to bringing the unrest to an end.
